claim: The local framework idea is best framed as a reusable execution/evaluation substrate plus app-specific adapters, not as another static benchmark.
evidence: The design separates a TaskAdapter from generic components: Golden Dataset Schema, Metric Registry, Eval Harness, Mutator, Archive, Convergence logic, and Guardrails.

claim: Promptfoo is the most mature production-style open-source eval tool found, focused on prompts, agents, RAG, red-teaming, local execution, CI/CD, and declarative configs.
evidence: README says it can test prompts/models, secure LLM apps with red teaming, compare providers, automate CI/CD, and run LLM evals locally.

claim: DeepEval maps closely to the metric-registry/testing part of the proposed framework, with Pytest-like unit testing for LLM apps and ready metrics for agents, RAG, chatbots, hallucination, answer relevancy, task completion, and tool correctness.
evidence: README describes DeepEval as an open-source framework similar to Pytest but specialized for unit testing LLM apps, with metrics including G-Eval, task completion, answer relevancy, and hallucination.

claim: Inspect AI is the strongest reference for a serious eval execution substrate, because it explicitly covers prompt engineering, tool usage, multi-turn dialog, model-graded evals, and extension packages.
evidence: README calls Inspect a framework from the UK AI Security Institute and lists built-in components for prompt engineering, tool usage, multi-turn dialog, and model graded evaluations.

claim: DSPy is the closest widely adopted system to the adapter-plus-optimizer concept when the system can be expressed as modular LM programs.
evidence: DSPy README says it is for programming rather than prompting language models and offers algorithms for optimizing prompts and weights for classifiers, RAG pipelines, and agent loops.

claim: Harness Evals is a small but directly relevant repo because it has normalized 0.0-1.0 scores and an iterative diagnose, rewrite, re-evaluate PromptOptimizer loop against a golden set.
evidence: README says every metric produces a normalized Score and its PromptOptimizer runs diagnose -> rewrite -> re-evaluate until target score or patience is exhausted, rejecting self-evaluation.

claim: Opendatahub agent-eval-harness is relevant for skill/agent execution ergonomics: it derives eval.yaml from a skill, runs cases, gathers artifacts, scores with judges, and supports runner permissions and cost/time controls.
evidence: README describes eval-analyze generating eval.yaml, eval-run preparing a workspace and collecting artifacts, and config fields for timeout, max budget, parallelism, runner, models, and permissions.

claim: The strongest arXiv support for the local architecture is the unified-agent-evaluation argument: standardize the surrounding harness/execution layer while letting benchmark content and goals vary.
evidence: The paper argues a unified framework should standardize the layer around the LLM, with a controlled execution substrate and principled evaluation methodology.

claim: The benchmark-quality literature supports the proposed guardrails: insufficient tests and broken reward designs can over/underestimate agent performance, and holdout/checklist discipline matters.
evidence: ABC paper reports benchmark setup/reward issues can cause up to 100% relative misestimation and introduces task validity, outcome validity, and reporting checks.

claim: OPRO is the canonical early pattern for LLM-as-optimizer loops: previous solutions plus values go into the prompt, the LLM proposes new solutions, then evaluation feeds the next round.
evidence: Abstract describes Optimization by Prompting, where each step prompts the LLM with previous solutions and values, evaluates generated solutions, and adds them to the next prompt.

claim: DSPy's paper supports optimizing arbitrary LM pipelines against a metric rather than hand-coded prompt templates.
evidence: Abstract introduces a programming model for LM pipelines as text-transformation graphs and a compiler that optimizes any DSPy pipeline to maximize a given metric.

claim: TextGrad supports the idea that optimizer feedback should be rich textual diagnostics, not only scalar scores.
evidence: Abstract says TextGrad backpropagates textual feedback from LLMs to improve components of compound AI systems and works across prompts, code, molecules, and more.

claim: GEPA is the closest research match to a mutator/archive/convergence runtime: it samples trajectories, reflects on failures, mutates prompts, and uses a Pareto frontier.
evidence: Abstract says GEPA samples trajectories, reflects in natural language, diagnoses problems, proposes prompt updates, and combines lessons from its Pareto frontier.

claim: GEPA's current GitHub implementation already exposes a general optimize_anything interface that can optimize text artifacts including code, architectures, configs, and SVGs via an evaluator.
evidence: README says optimize_anything optimizes any text artifact; users provide an evaluator and the system handles search.

claim: AutoPDL supports treating agent prompt choices as a structured AutoML problem over prompting patterns, demonstrations, and source-to-source readable programs.
evidence: Abstract frames prompt optimization as AutoML over agentic/non-agentic prompting patterns and demonstrations, producing readable executable PDL programs.

claim: Recent agent-optimization papers are moving beyond prompt-only loops toward full pipeline configuration tuning with benchmark outcomes and execution logs as feedback.
evidence: Artemis paper says it treats agents as black boxes, jointly optimizes textual and parametric components, captures interdependencies, and uses benchmark outcomes plus logs.

claim: Environment-grounded prompt optimization for game agents gives a concrete mutator design: decompose observation-to-action into modules and iteratively refine prompts through an LLM-driven evolutionary loop guided by returns.
evidence: Abstract introduces a framework decomposing the pipeline into descriptor and action-selection agents and refining prompts through an evolutionary loop guided by environment returns.

claim: As of the GitHub API check on 2026-07-07, high-adoption references were DSPy (~35.9k stars), promptfoo (~23.0k), DeepEval (~16.7k), GEPA (~5.5k), TextGrad (~3.6k), Inspect AI (~2.3k); niche direct harnesses had much lower stars.
evidence: GitHub API spot-check returned stars and recent push dates for the candidate repos during this run.
